text: I don't know of any such systems used outside of research. I hope to see such systems scale up.

Just realized that Realtalk in Dynamicland is exactly that pair of languages that I described. It has Managing Language with its Claim, When and Wish IO instructions. It has Lua as a Calculating Language, which code can be treated as a pure function by Managing Language.

attachments.fallback:
<https://twitter.com/vladimir_vg|@vladimir_vg>: A bit about programming languages.

It feels like there are two distinct programming languages that PL designers habitually mix into one.

1) Calculating language. It's code lives inside box with explicitly specified inputs and outputs. There is no IO, nor time inside the box.

text: Keep in mind they aren't wedded to the particular tech.  Lua is clean and small.  As for claiming and wishing, the goal is to make it so that there's very loose coupling between modules — or really sheets of paper.  One reason you don't see a lot written about Realtalk is that they don't want people mistaking it for Dynamicland.

Keep in mind they aren't wedded to the particular tech.  Lua is clean and small.  As for claiming and wishing, the goal is to make it so that there's very loose coupling between modules — or really sheets of paper.  One reason you don't see a lot written about Realtalk is that they don't want people mistaking it for Dynamicland.

Hello, I was recommended this slack on Hacker News. I am based in London, I'm a former programmer and keep it up in my spare time. I studied maths at uni. Website here <https://chris-knott.appspot.com>, GitHub here <https://github.com/ChrisKnott>

I am interested in why some clever people are put off programming but love other stuff like Excel. I particularly love things that make the abstract concrete.
<@U5TCAFTD3>, by any chance have you seen <http://www.hyperfiddle.net/>? I’m curious as to how it is similar/different from what you want to accomplish =)…
<@U5TCAFTD3>, by any chance have you seen <http://www.hyperfiddle.net/>? I’m curious as to how it is similar/different from what you want to accomplish =)…

Thanks <@UDPV407CZ>! This is a very cool platform I hadn't seen. Where did you find it?

It definitely is similar to what I'm looking to accomplish in many ways: it blurs the distinction between front and backend, allows for quick prototyping.

However I don't think it is trying to accomplish my goal of "allowing people who use software to edit that software in the course of using it". That is, it doesn't seem to create apps that are modifiable by their users. (Their model does seem quite simple so it would be easier for users to edit if they were given permissions.)

Additionally, I believe that often what people want to edit most about an app is its user interface, which is definitely not a focus here. My work is more focused on the user interface at the moment, and eventually I'll want to extend to the backend...

Does that answer your question?

Ok, just for some background in case it’s useful, I’m building a complex data visualiser for business/non-tech people. My friend who’s working on it with me is a business guy, so I regularly have to work out how to make it more accessible =)… We’ve got some users and I’m really very interested in making computation more accessible.

objects in the system can publish "claims" and "wishes", which are kind of like a generalized interface
objects in the system can publish "claims" and "wishes", which are kind of like a generalized interface
any object can read any claims and wishes of any other object in the room
any object can read any claims and wishes of any other object in the room
this was meant to make an ecosystem where one object didn't need to know a specialized "API" to communicate with other objects
this was meant to make an ecosystem where one object didn't need to know a specialized "API" to communicate with other objects
as Alan Kay quotes Licklider, this is the "communicating with aliens" problem — how do two entities with no known shared context figure out how to talk to each other
as Alan Kay quotes Licklider, this is the "communicating with aliens" problem — how do two entities with no known shared context figure out how to talk to each other
this was Licklider's vision of the internet, an ecosystem more like how biological organisms talk with each other
this was Licklider's vision of the internet, an ecosystem more like how biological organisms talk with each other
Bret Victor described a system kind of like this in Magic Ink: <http://worrydream.com/MagicInk/#engineering_inference_from_the_environment>
Bret Victor described a system kind of like this in Magic Ink: <http://worrydream.com/MagicInk/#engineering_inference_from_the_environment>
The claims and wishes system is genius and is meant to be a system that supports programs and humans interacting all together in one abstraction
The claims and wishes system is genius and is meant to be a system that supports programs and humans interacting all together in one abstraction

"claims" being facts about the world, like measurements or data, and "wishes" being how a human or program would like the world to be (with other programs free to interpret act on that wish and make it true if they have the ability)

Hey, I'm a software developer based in Bucharest. I'm not working on anything I could call the future of programming yet. Although, this project shows my interest with increasing developer efficiency. It's a command line interface for documentation: <http://cli.elbear.com/>

Other than that, I'm interested in exploring how tooling for functional languages could be improved. Specifically, I think Haskell could have hugely better tooling thanks to its immutability and pure functions. The link to the MPS tutorial has been sitting in a browser tab for a few months (<https://confluence.jetbrains.com/display/MPSD20181/Fast+Track+to+MPS>)
You may be interested in <https://www.cs.kent.ac.uk/projects/refactor-fp/> and one of the resulting artifacts, the (apparently actively maintained) Haskell Refactorer <https://github.com/alanz/HaRe>
thanks, Brian. I’ll have a look

New feature, "change time travel" (undo).

Slide through changes and see how they behave on current data.

It can be combined with tracing and input time travel to see how it behaves on previous data.

Any entry can be recovered and used as current.
